content.text: System-Assigned Managed Identity

A system-assigned managed identity is an Entra ID service principal that Azure automatically creates and ties to a single Azure resource. When you enable a system-assigned identity on a Virtual Machine, App Service, Azure Function, or other supported resource, Azure generates and manages the credential — there is no secret, password, or certificate for you to handle. When the resource is deleted, Azure automatically deletes the service principal.

attrs.alt: System-Assigned Managed Identity lifecycle: resource creation auto-creates SP, RBAC assignment grants access, app acquires token via IMDS, resource deletion removes SP

content.text: How System-Assigned Identities Work

The identity's lifecycle is inseparable from the resource's lifecycle. Azure manages a certificate for the service principal and rotates it automatically. Your code acquires tokens by calling the Instance Metadata Service (IMDS) on a link-local address that is only reachable from inside the Azure resource — not from the internet or from other virtual machines.

IMDS endpoint: http://169.254.169.254/metadata/identity/oauth2/token — only reachable from within the Azure resource that holds the identity. No credential is stored anywhere in your code or configuration.
One identity per resource — you cannot share a system-assigned identity between two resources.
The identity's name in Entra ID matches the Azure resource name.
Azure rotates the underlying certificate automatically; your code is never aware of the rotation.
Deleting the resource deletes the service principal and all its role assignments instantly.
Supported on: VMs, VM Scale Sets, App Service, Azure Functions, Container Instances, AKS (node pools), Logic Apps, Batch accounts, and many more.
Enabling System-Assigned Identity
Via Azure Portal
Navigate to your resource (e.g., a Virtual Machine).
In the left menu, select Identity under Security.
On the System assigned tab, switch Status to On.
Click Save. Azure creates a service principal and shows you its Object ID.
Via Azure CLI

# Enable system-assigned identity on an existing VM
az vm identity assign \
    --resource-group MyResourceGroup \
    --name MyVM

# Output includes the principalId (Entra ID object ID) and tenantId
# {
#   "systemAssignedIdentity": "<object-id>",
#   "userAssignedIdentities": {}
# }

# Enable on App Service
az webapp identity assign \
    --resource-group MyResourceGroup \
    --name MyWebApp

# Enable on Azure Function
az functionapp identity assign \
    --resource-group MyResourceGroup \
    --name MyFunctionApp
Via Azure PowerShell

# Enable system-assigned identity on a VM
$vm = Get-AzVM -ResourceGroupName 'MyResourceGroup' -Name 'MyVM'
Update-AzVM -ResourceGroupName 'MyResourceGroup' -VM $vm -IdentityType SystemAssigned

# Get the principal ID (Entra ID object ID of the SP)
$principalId = (Get-AzVM -ResourceGroupName 'MyResourceGroup' -Name 'MyVM').Identity.PrincipalId
Write-Host "Principal ID: $principalId"

# Enable on App Service
Set-AzWebApp -ResourceGroupName 'MyResourceGroup' -Name 'MyWebApp' -AssignIdentity $true
Via Bicep/ARM
Assigning an RBAC Role to the Identity

Enabling the identity creates the service principal, but it has no permissions until you assign RBAC roles. Always apply least privilege — grant only the role the resource needs on the narrowest scope (resource > resource group > subscription).

# Get the principal ID of the system-assigned identity
PRINCIPAL_ID=$(az vm show \
    --resource-group MyResourceGroup \
    --name MyVM \
    --query identity.principalId \
    --output tsv)

# Grant Key Vault Secrets User on a specific Key Vault
KV_ID=$(az keyvault show \
    --resource-group MyResourceGroup \
    --name MyKeyVault \
    --query id --output tsv)

az role assignment create \
    --assignee-object-id "$PRINCIPAL_ID" \
    --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \
    --role 'Key Vault Secrets User' \
    --scope "$KV_ID"

# Grant Storage Blob Data Reader on a specific storage account
STORAGE_ID=$(az storage account show \
    --resource-group MyResourceGroup \
    --name mystorageaccount \
    --query id --output tsv)

az role assignment create \
    --assignee-object-id "$PRINCIPAL_ID" \
    --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \
    --role 'Storage Blob Data Reader' \
    --scope "$STORAGE_ID"
Acquiring a Token at Runtime

Your application code acquires tokens by calling the IMDS endpoint. The Azure SDK (DefaultAzureCredential) does this automatically — you rarely need to call IMDS directly. When running outside Azure (local dev), DefaultAzureCredential falls back to Azure CLI, environment variables, or developer credentials.
Python — Azure SDK (Recommended)

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.keyvault.secrets import SecretClient

# DefaultAzureCredential automatically uses the system-assigned identity
# when running on an Azure resource with a managed identity enabled.
# Falls back to Azure CLI credentials in local development.
credential = DefaultAzureCredential()

kv_url = 'https://mykeyvault.vault.azure.net'
client = SecretClient(vault_url=kv_url, credential=credential)

secret = client.get_secret('my-database-password')
print(f'Secret value: {secret.value}')
Python — Direct IMDS Call (For Reference)

import requests

# Direct IMDS token acquisition — normally handled by Azure SDK
IMDS_URL = 'http://169.254.169.254/metadata/identity/oauth2/token'

params = {
    'api-version': '2018-02-01',
    'resource':    'https://vault.azure.net',   # target resource audience
}
headers = {'Metadata': 'true'}

response = requests.get(IMDS_URL, params=params, headers=headers, timeout=5)
token_data = response.json()
access_token = token_data['access_token']

# Use the token to call Key Vault directly
kv_resp = requests.get(
    'https://mykeyvault.vault.azure.net/secrets/my-secret?api-version=7.4',
    headers={'Authorization': f'Bearer {access_token}'},
)
print(kv_resp.json()['value'])
C# — Azure SDK
Terraform — System-Assigned Identity and Role Assignment
Verifying Identity Configuration (PowerShell)

Connect-AzAccount

# Verify identity is enabled on the VM
$vm = Get-AzVM -ResourceGroupName 'MyResourceGroup' -Name 'MyVM'
Write-Host 'Identity type:' $vm.Identity.Type
Write-Host 'Principal ID:' $vm.Identity.PrincipalId

# Verify the SP exists in Entra ID
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'Application.Read.All'
$sp = Get-MgServicePrincipal -Filter "id eq '$($vm.Identity.PrincipalId)'"
Write-Host 'SP display name:' $sp.DisplayName
Write-Host 'SP type:' $sp.ServicePrincipalType

# List role assignments for the identity
Get-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $vm.Identity.PrincipalId |
    Format-Table RoleDefinitionName, Scope
Common Use Cases
Scenario
Resource
Role Assigned
Target Scope
Read secrets from Key Vault
VM / App Service / Function
Key Vault Secrets User
Specific Key Vault
Read blobs from Storage
VM / Function / Container
Storage Blob Data Reader
Storage account or container
Write to Storage (outputs)
Function / Logic App
Storage Blob Data Contributor
Specific container
Send messages to Service Bus
VM / App Service
Azure Service Bus Data Sender
Specific queue or topic
Connect to Azure SQL
VM / App Service
db_datareader (SQL role)
SQL database
Pull images from ACR
AKS node pool / Container Instance
AcrPull
Azure Container Registry
Limitations and When to Use User-Assigned Instead
A system-assigned identity cannot be shared — if you have 10 VMs that all need the same Key Vault access, you must assign the role 10 times (one per VM's principal ID).

There is no pre-provisioning: the identity only exists once the resource exists. This can create circular dependency problems in infrastructure-as-code when the role assignment must exist before the resource starts.
If you delete and recreate the resource, the new identity has a different principal ID and you must re-create all role assignments.
For scenarios requiring a shared identity across multiple resources, or where the identity must be pre-created, use a user-assigned managed identity instead.
Using the Identity with Azure SQL (Passwordless Connection)

Azure SQL supports Entra ID authentication, allowing a system-assigned managed identity to connect without a password. You add the identity as a SQL user in the database and grant it the appropriate database role. The application uses DefaultAzureCredential to acquire a token for the SQL scope and passes it to the SQL driver.
Create the SQL User for the Identity
Connect from Python with Managed Identity

import struct, pyodbc
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

# Acquire token for Azure SQL
credential   = DefaultAzureCredential()
token_obj    = credential.get_token('https://database.windows.net/.default')
access_token = token_obj.token

# Convert token string to bytes for the SQL driver token attr
token_bytes = access_token.encode('utf-16-le')
token_struct = struct.pack(f'<I{len(token_bytes)}s', len(token_bytes), token_bytes)

SQL_COPT_SS_ACCESS_TOKEN = 1256  # pyodbc attribute constant
conn_str = (
    'DRIVER={ODBC Driver 18 for SQL Server};'
    'SERVER=myserver.database.windows.net;'
    'DATABASE=MyDatabase;'
    'Encrypt=yes;TrustServerCertificate=no;'
)
conn = pyodbc.connect(conn_str, attrs_before={SQL_COPT_SS_ACCESS_TOKEN: token_struct})
cursor = conn.cursor()
cursor.execute('SELECT TOP 5 name FROM sys.tables')
for row in cursor.fetchall():
    print(row.name)
Azure Functions — Accessing Key Vault Without a Connection String

Azure Functions with system-assigned identity is a common pattern for serverless workloads that need secrets. You can reference Key Vault secrets directly in the function's app settings using the @Microsoft.KeyVault() reference syntax — no SDK code required.
Key Vault Reference in App Settings

# 1. Enable system-assigned identity on the Function App
az functionapp identity assign \
    --resource-group MyResourceGroup \
    --name MyFunctionApp

# 2. Get the principal ID
PRINCIPAL_ID=$(az functionapp identity show \
    --resource-group MyResourceGroup \
    --name MyFunctionApp \
    --query principalId --output tsv)

# 3. Grant Key Vault Secrets User
KV_ID=$(az keyvault show --name MyKeyVault --query id --output tsv)
az role assignment create \
    --assignee-object-id "$PRINCIPAL_ID" \
    --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \
    --role 'Key Vault Secrets User' \
    --scope "$KV_ID"

# 4. Set app setting using Key Vault reference syntax
# The function host resolves this at startup — no code change needed
az functionapp config appsettings set \
    --resource-group MyResourceGroup \
    --name MyFunctionApp \
    --settings "DB_PASSWORD=@Microsoft.KeyVault(SecretUri=https://mykeyvault.vault.azure.net/secrets/db-password/)"

Key Vault references in app settings resolve at startup. The function code reads os.environ['DB_PASSWORD'] and gets the secret value — it never sees the @Microsoft.KeyVault(...) syntax. If the managed identity lacks access, the function fails to start with a 'Key Vault reference resolution failed' error.
KQL: Auditing Managed Identity Usage
Sign-In Activity for a Managed Identity

// Track sign-ins by managed identities in the last 30 days
// Managed identity sign-ins use ServicePrincipal servicePrincipalType
AADServicePrincipalSignInLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(30d)
| where ServicePrincipalName contains 'MyVM'   // or filter by ServicePrincipalId
| project TimeGenerated, ServicePrincipalName, ResourceDisplayName, ResultType, IPAddress
| order by TimeGenerated desc
Role Assignment Changes for Managed Identities

// Detect when a managed identity's role assignments changed
AuditLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(7d)
| where OperationName in ('Add member to role', 'Remove member from role')
| where TargetResources[0].displayName contains 'MyVM'
| extend AssignedBy  = InitiatedBy.user.userPrincipalName
| extend RoleName    = tostring(TargetResources[1].displayName)
| extend MIName      = tostring(TargetResources[0].displayName)
| project TimeGenerated, OperationName, MIName, RoleName, AssignedBy
| order by TimeGenerated desc
Disabling or Removing a System-Assigned Identity

Disabling a system-assigned identity removes the service principal from Entra ID and all role assignments, but leaves the resource intact. This is useful for temporarily revoking a resource's access without deleting it.

# Disable the system-assigned identity (removes SP + role assignments)
az vm identity remove \
    --resource-group MyResourceGroup \
    --name MyVM \
    --identities '[system]'

# Verify: identity should now show as 'None'
az vm show \
    --resource-group MyResourceGroup \
    --name MyVM \
    --query identity

# Disable via PowerShell
$vm = Get-AzVM -ResourceGroupName 'MyResourceGroup' -Name 'MyVM'
Update-AzVM -ResourceGroupName 'MyResourceGroup' -VM $vm -IdentityType None
Write-Host 'System-assigned identity removed.'
Troubleshooting
Symptom
Root Cause
Fix
DefaultAzureCredential: 'No credential providers succeeded'
Identity not enabled, or app running locally without CLI login
Enable identity in portal/CLI; run 'az login' for local dev; check AZURE_CLIENT_ID env var
403 Forbidden when calling Key Vault
Identity enabled but RBAC role not assigned
Assign 'Key Vault Secrets User' (RBAC) or 'get' access policy (legacy) to the principal ID
Key Vault reference failed to resolve at startup
Identity lacks 'Key Vault Secrets User'; or secret name/version incorrect
Check the role assignment; verify the secret URI matches exactly including trailing slash
IMDS call times out (connection refused)
Code is running outside Azure (local machine)
Use DefaultAzureCredential which falls back to Azure CLI; never call IMDS from outside Azure
Old principal ID after recreating resource
System-assigned identity is re-created with a new principal ID
Re-create all role assignments; update any policy bindings that reference the old ID
Summary
System-assigned managed identities are the simplest form of Azure workload identity — Azure creates and deletes the service principal automatically with the resource.
Enable via the portal, CLI, PowerShell, Bicep/ARM, or Terraform by setting identity.type = SystemAssigned.
Azure creates a service principal in Entra ID with the same name as the resource and manages the certificate automatically.
Assign RBAC roles on the narrowest scope needed — Key Vault Secrets User, Storage Blob Data Reader, AcrPull, etc.
Applications call IMDS (169.254.169.254) or use DefaultAzureCredential; no secrets or connection strings in code.
The identity is deleted automatically when the resource is deleted — no orphaned service principals.
Use user-assigned identities when you need to share an identity across resources or pre-provision before the resource exists.
